does: This paper introduces ProGenMech, a mechanistic interpretability framework that extends cross-layer transcoders to analyze how generative protein language models like ProGen3 produce novel protein sequences. The framework reconstructs intermediate representations across all layers using sparse latent variables, capturing inter-layer computation rather than just individual layers. The authors develop a zero-shot circuit discovery method to identify sparse latent circuits responsible for both causal generation and fitness prediction tasks, demonstrating that their approach outperforms local transformer attribution methods in explaining generative behavior and fitness estimation.
connects: This work builds on the mechanistic interpretability literature that has applied sparse autoencoders and transcoders to understand protein representation learning, extending these techniques from static models to the autoregressive generation setting where most prior work focused on how models encode protein information rather than how they generate it. It combines cross-layer transcoder approaches that capture inter-layer dependencies with circuit analysis methods from broader mechanistic interpretability research, adapting these tools specifically for Mixture-of-Experts architectures. The paper responds to a gap in understanding generative protein models, where previous sparse coding methods proved insufficient for capturing the causal computation required during sequence generation.
enables: This framework opens pathways for improving protein generation by identifying and manipulating the internal circuits responsible for desired properties, potentially enabling more direct editing of generative models without retraining. The zero-shot circuit discovery approach could facilitate rapid diagnosis of failure modes in protein generation and explain how models extrapolate beyond natural sequences. Mechanistically understanding generative computation in protein models may accelerate applications in synthetic biology and drug discovery by clarifying which circuit components drive generation of functional proteins, and the methodology could generalize to other biological sequence models or multimodal generative systems.
